title: Open House Praha 2026 — Modernist & Brutalist Picks
taste: Functionalism, Bauhaus, brutalism, mid-century modernism. Mies / Corbusier / Wright / Breuer / Eames / Rams.

ranked.name: Veletržní palác – Národní galerie Praha
ranked.why: The single most important functionalist building in the country (1928, Tyl + Fuchs). When Le Corbusier visited in 1928 he allegedly said "I was astonished. I saw architecture, the architecture I've been thinking about." Basically Prague's manifesto answer to everything on your list.
ranked.notToMiss: The grand seven-story atrium with its glass-block floors. The original elevators and brass details. Inside: Mucha's Slav Epic and the Czech modern art collection (Kupka, Filla, Toyen).

ranked.name: Kongresové centrum Praha
ranked.why: The Palace of Culture (1981). Late-socialist brutalism at its most monumental — bronze, travertine, raw concrete, endless geometric repetition. The kind of naked-concrete wall you said you love, scaled up to a city block.
ranked.notToMiss: The main 2,800-seat hall, the foyer's marble + bronze + glass cosmos, and the terrace view back across Vyšehrad and the Nusle valley.

ranked.name: Národní divadlo – provozní budova (Nová scéna)
ranked.why: Karel Prager's 1983 brutalist insertion next to the gold-roofed National Theatre. The dialogue between cast-concrete grid and the historic neo-renaissance is exactly the old/new tension you don't get to see often.
ranked.notToMiss: The glass-brick façade of the Nová scéna (looks completely different lit from inside at dusk), Prager's exposed structural concrete in the back-of-house spaces, the rooftop.

ranked.name: Hotel Fairmont Golden Prague (ex-Intercontinental)
ranked.why: Karel Filsak's 1974 Intercontinental — Prague's most famous brutalist hotel, just reopened after the Fairmont conversion. Bronze, travertine, concrete relief panels. The whole 1970s government-luxury brutalism mood.
ranked.notToMiss: Whatever original Filsak detailing survived the renovation — the lobby relief work, the bar levels, the riverside terraces. Open House is the rare moment you can see it without booking a room.

ranked.name: Palác Škodových závodů
ranked.why: Pavel Janák, 1926. Janák is the architect Czech modernism revolves around, and this is one of his cleanest functionalist works. Now Magistrát hl. m. Prahy.
ranked.notToMiss: The grand staircase, the original elevator cores, the period office interiors that survived the conversion to city hall.

ranked.name: Trmalova vila
ranked.why: Your Wright pick. Jan Kotěra's 1903 villa — the father of Czech modern architecture absorbing exactly the Wright / Prairie school influences you fell for in Fallingwater. Built-in furniture, horizontal masses, integration with landscape.
ranked.notToMiss: The original Kotěra-designed built-in furniture and lighting fixtures still in situ. This is essentially a Czech Robie House, in miniature.

ranked.name: Národní archiv a Státní oblastní archiv v Praze
ranked.why: The Chodovec archive complex — late socialist brutalism, finished 1990s but designed under normalization. Pure modular concrete, fortress-like volumes, the function-equals-form ethos applied to storing the nation's paper.
ranked.notToMiss: The storage stacks tour (rarely open), the central reading room, the exterior modular concrete geometry from the access road.

ranked.name: Budova podniku zahraničního obchodu Strojimport
ranked.why: 1970s socialist-modernist headquarters of the state machinery export company. The kind of late-modernist corporate building Western Europe largely lost — Prague kept theirs.
ranked.notToMiss: Original lobby interior, conference rooms, façade detailing. Period furniture and signage if it survived.

ranked.name: Areál Jinonice – Fakulta sociálních věd UK
ranked.why: A late-socialist university campus (originally a Communist Party training school), now Charles University's social-science faculty. Monumental concrete pavilions on an axial plan — utopian-brutalist campus planning.
ranked.notToMiss: The axial spine between buildings, the lecture hall interiors, the rough concrete fenestration patterns.

ranked.name: Ministerstvo dopravy ČR
ranked.why: The former Czechoslovak Railways HQ on the embankment — a major interwar functionalist palace, normally closed because it's a working ministry.
ranked.notToMiss: The cavernous central hall, the period ministerial offices, the riverside façade rhythm.

ranked.name: Palác Dunaj
ranked.why: Adolf Foehr, 1929. Pure Národní-třída functionalism — a clean corner block with ribbon windows and the kind of restrained street presence Mies would have approved of.
ranked.notToMiss: The corner, the original passageway through the block, the entrance lobby.

ranked.name: Palác Atlas – kino
ranked.why: Functionalist palace with one of the era's iconic cinema interiors. Cinemas are where Czech functionalism lets itself become theatrical — curves, indirect lighting, period typography.
ranked.notToMiss: The cinema hall itself if accessible — even the foyer is worth it.

ranked.name: Klubový dům Autoklubu České republiky
ranked.why: 1920s clubhouse of the Czech Auto Club — transitional functionalism with the era's love of cars baked into the brief. Members-only normally.
ranked.notToMiss: The clubrooms, the bar, period petrol-head ephemera that's sometimes on display.

ranked.name: Hlavův ústav
ranked.why: Alois Špalek, 1921. Early functionalist / late modernist Institute of Pathology — the moment Czech architecture stops being decorated and starts being geometric.
ranked.notToMiss: The lecture amphitheater, the corridor proportions, the staircase.

ranked.name: Filosofický ústav, Sociologický ústav a Ústav dějin umění AV ČR
ranked.why: Academy of Sciences functionalist building by Bohumír Kozák. Restrained, scholarly, almost Bauhaus-quiet.
ranked.notToMiss: The reading rooms, the library stacks, the inner courtyard.

ranked.name: Palác Skaut – Divadlo Minor
ranked.why: 1930s functionalist palace built for the Scouts, now home to the Minor children's theatre. Crisp interwar volumes on Senovážné náměstí.
ranked.notToMiss: The theatre hall interior, the original staircase, the rooftop terrace if open.

ranked.name: Palác Adria
ranked.why: Pavel Janák + Josef Zasche, 1922–25. Not pure functionalism — this is Rondo-cubism, the brief Czech "national style" between cubism and functionalism. More decorative than your usual taste, but so specifically Czech avant-garde that an architecture lover shouldn't skip it.
ranked.notToMiss: The internal passageway, the Adria theatre, the rooftop ceramic ornament.

ranked.name: Palác Poštovního šekového úřadu
ranked.why: The 1929 Postal Cheque Office palace — one of the Czech functionalist set-pieces, built when the new republic was inventing modern bureaucracy and gave it a modern face.
ranked.notToMiss: The grand banking hall — vast, top-lit, period clocks and counters.

ranked.name: Sokolovna T. J. Sokol Libeň
ranked.why: Functionalist Sokol gymnasium — the Sokol movement built dozens of these across Czech towns in the 1920s–30s as muscular, healthy-body modernism.
ranked.notToMiss: The main gym hall — usually a single big functionalist volume with skylights.

ranked.name: Ústřední ředitelství Československé tabákové režie – Městský soud v Praze
ranked.why: Late-1920s functionalist HQ of the state tobacco monopoly, now the Prague Municipal Court. Almost never publicly accessible.
ranked.notToMiss: The central courtroom, the original director's office levels, the staircase.

ranked.name: Palác Archa
ranked.why: Functionalist commercial palace on Na Poříčí — the area that has the densest concentration of interwar functionalism in Prague.
ranked.notToMiss: Façade detailing, lobby, the rooftop view across Poříčí's other functionalist palaces.

ranked.name: Palác Metro
ranked.why: Functionalist palace on Národní třída. Pair with key=101 (Divadlo Image) — once you're at one, do both, they're the same building.
ranked.notToMiss: Façade rhythm, the passage through the block.

ranked.name: Palác Metro – Divadlo Image
ranked.why: The theatre half of Palác Metro — black-light theatre inside a functionalist palace, an unusual combination.
ranked.notToMiss: The theatre hall interior.

ranked.name: Hasičský dům
ranked.why: Functionalist Firefighters' House. Functionalism applied to civic infrastructure — exactly the form-follows-function ideology in built form.
ranked.notToMiss: Original fire-engine bays if preserved, the staff floors above, period signage.

ranked.name: Gymnázium Na Zatlance
ranked.why: 1937 functionalist gymnasium — one of the last pre-war school buildings, by which point Czech architects had absolutely nailed the Bauhaus school typology.
ranked.notToMiss: The classroom corridor proportions, the aula, the original windows and ironmongery.

ranked.name: Budova Fakulty humanitních studií Univerzity Karlovy
ranked.why: Wildcard: this is the 2020 Kuba & Pilař building — contemporary, but explicitly neo-brutalist. Exposed cast-in-place concrete, monastery-like cloister logic. If you love a naked concrete wall, this is the new generation.
ranked.notToMiss: The central atrium with the concrete coffered ceiling, the cloistered courtyards, the library.

ranked.name: Velký strahovský stadion
ranked.why: The largest stadium in the world by area — mass-gymnastics monumentalism. Less Bauhaus-pure, more interwar-monumental, but the scale is hard to comprehend until you stand in it.
ranked.notToMiss: Walking the field itself; the underground locker tunnels if accessible.

ranked.name: Plovoucí loděnice TJ Tatran Praha
ranked.why: A floating functionalist boathouse on the Vltava. Niche, charming, totally on-genre for form follows function applied to water sports.
ranked.notToMiss: The structure from the water level, the changing rooms upstairs, the river view.

ranked.name: Vršovická vodárna v Michli
ranked.why: Interwar water tower / pumping station — functionalist civil-engineering aesthetic that you basically can't see from inside any other way.
ranked.notToMiss: The pump hall machinery, the tower ascent.

ranked.name: Vodárenská věž Letná
ranked.why: Older brick water tower at Letná. Not modernist per se, but the bare-engineering aesthetic and the view fit your appetite. Pair with Strahovský stadion since they're nearby.
ranked.notToMiss: The view from the top across the Vltava bend toward the Old Town.

ranked.name: Pragovka Art District
ranked.why: Adaptive reuse of the Praga car/truck factory — industrial-functionalist sheds and concrete halls now hosting artists. Not architecture-as-design but architecture-as-found-object.
ranked.notToMiss: The biggest hall, the surviving overhead cranes, whatever exhibitions are up.

ranked.name: Branický pivovar – Dům tanečního umění
ranked.why: Late-19th-c. brewery converted to a dance house — industrial brick + steel, similar appeal to Pragovka.
ranked.notToMiss: The main fermentation hall now used as performance space.

ranked.name: Velvyslanectví Litevské republiky v ČR
ranked.why: Embassies are rare-access. This one (Pod Klikovkou) sits in an interwar functionalist villa — combine architecture-tourism with embassy-tourism.
ranked.notToMiss: The villa interiors that are normally closed, the period staircase.

ranked.name: CAMP – Sdružení projektových ateliérů
ranked.why: Late-socialist building of the city planning institute, now the Centre for Architecture and Metropolitan Planning. The building is fine; the content (models of Prague, current architectural exhibitions) is what makes it worth a visit for someone with your interests.
ranked.notToMiss: The big city model in the main hall, the current temporary exhibition, the reading room.

advice: If you only get one day: lock in #1 (Veletržní palác) and #2 (Kongresové centrum) — together they cover the two poles of your taste (interwar functionalism + late socialist brutalism). Then fill the day with whichever of #3–#10 are geographically closest.

cancelled.name: Všeobecná fakultní nemocnice v Praze (key=248)
cancelled.note: Marked "budova zrušena" in the source list — building cancelled, don't plan around it.

skip.note: Stunning buildings but outside your taste cluster (baroque / gothic / neorenaissance / generic-new-office / historic-hospital wings).

categories.label: Historicist / neorenaissance villas and town halls
Generic new-build offices / residential
examples: Rezidenční projekt Fragment / Lihovar / Mint Living

categories.label: Industrial / railway (unless you're a train person)
Edge cases worth knowing
examples: Dům Diamant (key=255): 1913 cubist (Emil Králíček) — Czech avant-garde precursor. Slot near rank ~20 if cubist-to-functionalist arc interests you.
Nová libeňská synagoga (key=144): 1846 synagogue, architecturally minor.
